title: 4 2 Polar Coordinates
body:
4.2 DOUBLE INTEGRALS IN POLAR COORDINATES


      To transform from polar coordinates (r,θ) to Cartesian coordinates (x,y), use

                                x = r cos θ,        y = r sin θ.

      To transform from Cartesian coordinates (x,y) to polar coordinates (r,θ), use
                                  p                          y
                             r=    x2 + y 2 ,       θ = tan−1 .
                                                             x
                      The infinitesimal area in polar coordinates is

                                         dA = r dr dθ


Sometimes integrals become much simpler when expressed in terms of the polar coordi-
nates (r, θ) rather than the usual Cartesian coordinates (x, y). This is particularly true
when dealing with circular objects or sums of squares.
Let us begin by defining polar coordinates and proving the transformations above.




                                                           dA
                                                    l=r

                                                       d




                                     d      r



The shaded area dA is approximately a rectangle and hence its area is given by

                                  dA ≈ (rdθ)dr = rdrdθ

                                                 5
                          Jacobian Transformation (Optional)




The equation dA = dxdy = rdrdθ is a special case of a Jacobian transformation. If
x = x(u, v), y = y(u, v) is a change of variable in a double integral then
                   Z Z                   Z Z
                         f (x, y)dx dy =      f (x(u, v), y(u, v)) |J|du dv
                     Ω                    Ω∗

where Ω∗ is the region in the (u, v) plane corresponding to Ω in the (x, y) plane and J is
the Jacobian determinant
                                    ∂x   ∂x
                                    ∂u   ∂v
                                                   ∂x ∂y ∂x ∂y
                             J=     ∂y   ∂y    =        −      .
                                    ∂u   ∂v        ∂u ∂v ∂v ∂u

For polar coordinates, x = r cos(θ) and y = r sin(θ) then:

      ∂x   ∂x
                    ∂x ∂y ∂x ∂y
J=    ∂r
      ∂y
           ∂θ
           ∂y   =        −      = cos(θ)r cos(θ)+r sin(θ) sin(θ) = r(cos2 (θ)+sin2 (θ)) = r.
      ∂r   ∂θ       ∂r ∂θ ∂θ ∂r

Hence, dA = r dr dθ.




We will not deal with triple integrals in this course, but this result is easily extended to
three dimensions.


                                               6
 Example 8 (Optional) The spherical polar coordinates express the position of a point
in terms of r, the radius, θ, the angle with the x-axis, and φ, the angle with the z-axis,

                       x = r cos θ sin φ,   y = r sin θ sin φ,   z = r cos φ.

Calculate the Jacobian determinant for this coordinate system and show that the infinites-
imal volume for spherical polar coordinates is

                              dV = dx dy dz = r2 sin φ dr dθ dφ.
